findings.reference_source: Tokio best practices: blocking I/O must not run on the async executor thread
findings.issue: load_project_instructions() and load_project_instructions_with_config() use blocking std::fs I/O (std::fs::read_to_string, walkdir, std::fs::read_dir) and are called directly from the async AgentStack::run() method in stack_run.rs (line 203). This blocks the tokio executor thread during filesystem traversal of potentially many directories (ancestor walk, rules glob, skill dirs).
findings.recommendation: Either (a) wrap the call in tokio::task::spawn_blocking, (b) convert the functions to async using tokio::fs, or (c) cache the result at startup and read from an Arc<RwLock<_>>. Option (c) is best since instructions don't change mid-run. Goose preloads MOIM context similarly at session start.

findings.reference_source: Rust error handling best practices (thiserror docs, Goose reference: uses anyhow+thiserror together for library vs binary code)
findings.issue: AvaError has a dual-personality design: structured variants (MissingApiKey, RateLimited, ToolNotFound, etc.) coexist with 10 legacy string-payload variants (ToolError(String), IoError(String), DatabaseError(String), etc.). The legacy variants erase the original error type information — e.g., sqlx::Error is converted to DatabaseError(e.to_string()) which loses the error kind, source chain, and machine-inspectable metadata. The 'from' impls only cover std::io::Error and serde_json::Error, so callers must manually .to_string() all other error types.
findings.recommendation: Add From<sqlx::Error> for AvaError (wrapping into DatabaseError with the typed sqlx error kind preserved), From<reqwest::Error> for AvaError, and continue migrating legacy string variants to structured variants. Consider using #[from] attribute on thiserror variants for transparent delegation. Long-term: library crates should use their own typed errors and convert to AvaError at the boundary.

findings.reference_source: Tokio best practices: detached tasks (fire-and-forget tokio::spawn) can panic silently and leave state inconsistent. Goose uses explicit JoinHandle tracking.
findings.issue: Multiple tokio::spawn calls throughout ava-agent (stack/mod.rs:141 for codebase indexing), ava-cli-providers, ava-config/model_catalog, and ava-tui/state/agent.rs spawn tasks with no JoinHandle retention. If these tasks panic, the error is silently dropped. The codebase indexing spawn in AgentStack::new() (stack/mod.rs:141) is the most critical — if it panics, the agent silently loses code search capability with no user-facing error.
findings.recommendation: Retain JoinHandles for background tasks. For the codebase indexer, store the JoinHandle in AgentStack and expose a method to await completion or check status. For fire-and-forget tasks where panics are truly acceptable, add a .inspect_err() tracing log. Use tokio::task::JoinSet for groups of related tasks.

findings.reference_source: Rust error handling best practices: From impls should cover all external error types that propagate through the system
findings.issue: AvaError only has From<std::io::Error> and From<serde_json::Error> implemented. Missing From impls for sqlx::Error (the primary database error type), reqwest::Error (HTTP client), toml::de::Error (config parsing), and serde_yaml::Error. All callers must manually call .to_string() to convert these, erasing error metadata.
findings.recommendation: Add From implementations for the major external error types: impl From<sqlx::Error> for AvaError, impl From<reqwest::Error> for AvaError. This would eliminate the pattern of .map_err(|e| AvaError::DatabaseError(e.to_string())) throughout the codebase.

findings.reference_source: Rust async best practices: excessive RwLock field usage in long-lived async structs; Goose uses message passing for state mutation
findings.issue: AgentStack has 9 separate RwLock fields (provider_override, model_override, routing_locked, thinking_level, mode_prompt_suffix, plan_mode, parent_session_id, mcp, codebase_index, disabled_mcp_servers, plus an Arc<tokio::sync::Mutex<PluginManager>>). Multiple read locks are acquired in sequence within async fn run() without a documented locking order. This is a latent deadlock risk if the acquisition order ever reverses, and creates overhead from 9+ lock acquisitions per run.
findings.recommendation: Consolidate mutable runtime state into a single Arc<RwLock<AgentRuntimeState>> struct. This reduces lock acquisitions from N to 1, ensures consistent lock ordering, and makes the state machine explicit. Alternatively, use a single-writer actor pattern (mpsc channel) for state mutations.
findings.code_snippet:
// 9 separate RwLock fields on AgentStack
provider_override: RwLock<Option<String>>,
model_override: RwLock<Option<String>>,
routing_locked: RwLock<bool>,
thinking_level: RwLock<ThinkingLevel>,
mode_prompt_suffix: RwLock<Option<String>>,
plan_mode: RwLock<bool>,
parent_session_id: RwLock<Option<String>>,
mcp: Arc<RwLock<Option<MCPRuntime>>>,
codebase_index: Arc<RwLock<Option<Arc<CodebaseIndex>>>>,

findings.reference_source: Tokio graceful shutdown best practices: SIGTERM is not handled on Linux/macOS; only SIGINT (ctrl+c) is caught
findings.issue: The headless mode only installs a ctrl+c handler (tokio::signal::ctrl_c) but does not handle SIGTERM. When AVA runs in Docker, Kubernetes, or as a systemd service, SIGTERM is the standard termination signal. Missing SIGTERM handling means the agent will not shut down gracefully in containerized/orchestrated deployments.
findings.recommendation: Use tokio::select! to await both ctrl_c and SIGTERM signals. On Linux, add signal(SignalKind::terminate()) from tokio::signal::unix. The TUI app mode also lacks SIGTERM handling.

findings.reference_source: Rust testing best practices: test helpers should use Result<T> and ? operator rather than .unwrap() to produce useful failure messages
findings.issue: Test helper functions like write_credentials() and write_routing_config() in stack_test.rs use .unwrap() directly, producing opaque panics on failure. This is a widespread pattern in test helpers across the codebase. While acceptable in tests, the pattern means test failures surface as 'called `Option::unwrap()` on a `None` value' rather than actionable messages.
findings.recommendation: Use the expect() method with descriptive messages instead of unwrap() in test helpers, or use the ? operator with #[tokio::test] functions returning Result<()>. The insta snapshot testing library is already a dev-dependency in ava-tui but is not yet used for regression testing of agent outputs.

findings.reference_source: Rust best practices: error propagation should not be mixed with .unwrap_or_default() for credential loading in CLI entry points
findings.issue: CredentialStore::load_default().await.unwrap_or_default() in the auth flow silently swallows credential file parse errors. If the credentials.json is malformed, the user gets no error — they just appear to have no credentials. This is a user-facing bug that is hard to diagnose.
findings.recommendation: Use .unwrap_or_else(|e| { eprintln!("Warning: could not load credentials: {e}"); Default::default() }) to at least surface the error, or propagate it with ? and handle it in the caller.

findings.reference_source: Rust workspace best practices: shared dependencies should be deduplicated via workspace.dependencies; individual crates should not re-pin transitive deps
findings.issue: The workspace Cargo.toml pins rust-version = "1.75" (released November 2023) while the codebase uses tokio_util::sync::CancellationToken, async-trait, and other features that work better on newer editions. Goose targets rust-version = "1.83" matching the stable release at time of development. The 1.75 pin may prevent adoption of stabilized async fn in traits (AFIT, stable in 1.75) and may cause confusion since async_trait is still used everywhere despite AFIT being available.
findings.recommendation: Bump rust-version to at least 1.80 (or current stable - 2). Remove async-trait from crates that could use AFIT directly (small performance benefit, better IDE support). Update workspace.lints.rust to include missing_docs = "warn" to improve API documentation coverage.
findings.code_snippet:
# Cargo.toml workspace
rust-version = "1.75"  # Nov 2023 — consider 1.80+ for AFIT
async-trait = "0.1"     # Still needed but could be reduced

findings.reference_source: Goose reference (same Rust+Tauri stack): uses proptest for property testing of agent configuration, OpenCode uses table-driven tests
findings.issue: No property-based testing (proptest, quickcheck, or bolero) anywhere in the workspace. The message queue, token counting, context compaction, and tool parameter validation are all ideal candidates for property testing — these are pure functions with well-defined input/output invariants that are hard to cover exhaustively with handwritten tests.
findings.recommendation: Add proptest as a dev-dependency at the workspace level. Priority targets: (1) ava-context token counting and compaction invariants, (2) ava-agent message queue ordering invariants (steering/follow-up/post-complete tiering), (3) ava-tools tool parameter validation with arbitrary JSON inputs.

findings.reference_source: Rust testing best practices: insta snapshot testing is already a dev-dependency but is unused
findings.issue: The insta crate is listed as a dev-dependency in ava-tui but no snapshot tests exist in the codebase. Snapshot testing is especially valuable for TUI rendering output (verifying that the rendered output of message bubbles, status bars, and modal dialogs does not regress), and for agent system prompt generation (verifying that build_system_prompt() output doesn't change unexpectedly).
findings.recommendation: Implement snapshot tests for: (1) system_prompt::build_system_prompt() output, (2) key TUI widget rendering using ratatui's testing utilities (TestBackend), (3) StreamChunk serialization format. This is low-effort since insta is already a dependency.

findings.reference_source: Rust workspace lints best practices: missing_docs, clippy::pedantic, unreachable_pub are standard in production Rust codebases
findings.issue: The workspace lints config has 10 clippy lints but is missing several high-value ones: missing_docs is not enforced (many public APIs lack doc comments), clippy::pedantic is not enabled, and there is no deny for clippy::unwrap_in_result or clippy::expect_in_result which would catch the pattern of .expect() in fallible functions. The unreachable_pub lint is also absent.
findings.recommendation: Add to [workspace.lints.clippy]: missing_docs = "warn" (or "deny"), clippy::unwrap_in_result = "warn", clippy::expect_used = "warn" (in library crates). Add missing_docs = "warn" to [workspace.lints.rust]. This aligns with Goose's clippy.toml which enforces these.
findings.code_snippet:
# Cargo.toml - missing high-value lints
[workspace.lints.clippy]
# todo = "warn"  ← already present
# missing: unwrap_in_result, expect_used, missing_docs

findings.reference_source: Rust lazy_static/once_cell best practices: LazyLock is stable since 1.80; LazyLock + Regex.unwrap() is a valid pattern but error messages could be improved
findings.issue: Regex patterns in ava-praxis/src/review.rs are compiled via LazyLock with .n() (unwrap) — if the regex is ever invalid, the panic message contains no context about which regex failed. Additionally, the regexes are complex patterns that would benefit from compile-time validation using the regex-lite or regex crate's proc_macro2 feature.
findings.recommendation: Add descriptive .expect("<regex_name> regex is valid") messages to the LazyLock regex initializers. Consider using the once_cell::sync::Lazy pattern with explicit error logging, or validate regexes in unit tests.

findings.reference_source: Reference implementations (Goose, OpenCode): integration tests against real providers use dedicated test fixtures and wiremock; AVA lacks provider-level integration tests
findings.issue: The ava-llm/tests/providers.rs file exists but contains only unit tests for provider creation. There are no integration tests that verify streaming SSE parsing, tool call parsing, token usage extraction, or thinking/reasoning content handling across the 8 providers. Goose has goose-test-support and dedicated integration test infrastructure.
findings.recommendation: Add wiremock or httpmock as dev-dependencies for provider tests. Create fixture-based tests that replay recorded API responses (saved as JSON files) through the streaming parsers. This would catch regressions in SSE chunk parsing without requiring live API keys in CI.

findings.reference_source: Rust CLI best practices: std::env::current_dir().unwrap_or_default() silently falls back to "." which may not be the expected behavior
findings.issue: Multiple places use std::env::current_dir().unwrap_or_default() which silently substitutes an empty PathBuf if the process can't determine its working directory. An empty PathBuf treated as "." could cause the agent to index or trust the wrong directory without any indication to the user that something went wrong.
findings.recommendation: Use std::env::current_dir().expect("cannot determine working directory") in places where a fallback is not acceptable, or explicitly log a warning when the fallback is used. At minimum, document that the working directory is critical to agent behavior.

findings.reference_source: OpenCode reference: configures stream timeout per-provider based on model characteristics; Goose externalizes timeout as a recipe parameter
findings.issue: LLM_STREAM_TIMEOUT_SECS = 90 is a hard-coded constant. While it is stored in AgentConfig.stream_timeout_secs, it is always initialized to the constant value and there is no per-provider or per-model timeout configuration. Thinking models (o3, Opus with extended thinking) may legitimately take 90-120s between tokens, causing premature timeouts. Non-thinking models rarely need more than 30s.
findings.recommendation: Add per-provider timeout configuration to the LLM provider configuration (e.g., config.llm.providers.anthropic.stream_timeout_secs). The timeout should default to 90s for thinking-capable models and 30s for standard models. Expose it in AgentStackConfig so headless mode can override it.

findings.reference_source: Rust CI best practices: nextest profile 'ci' should set fail-fast = false and retries for flaky async tests; MIRI tests for unsafe code are standard in production Rust
findings.issue: The CI workflow runs cargo nextest with --profile ci but there is no .config/nextest.toml defining the CI profile (or it's empty). The nextest configuration file would allow setting retry counts for flaky tests, timeout overrides, and test partitioning. Additionally, there is no MIRI or address sanitizer run to catch UB in unsafe blocks (the codebase has unsafe_code = "deny" which is excellent, but async unsafe patterns are harder for clippy to catch).
findings.recommendation: Create .config/nextest.toml with a [profile.ci] section that sets retries = 2 for flaky network tests. Add a separate CI job that runs cargo nextest --profile ci with a subset of critical tests under MIRI. The deny(unsafe_code) at workspace level is already good practice.
findings.code_snippet:
# .config/nextest.toml (missing)
[profile.ci]
retries = { backoff = "exponential", count = 2, delay = "1s" }
fail-fast = false

findings.reference_source: Rust best practices: unreachable!() in production match arms is a panic risk if new variants are added
findings.issue: Multiple unreachable!() calls in the create_provider() match arms (lines ~311, ~316) within the zai-coding-plan/zhipuai-coding-plan and alibaba/kimi/minimax branch arms. While logically correct (they appear inside guards that already matched the outer arm), if the guard logic ever changes, these become live panics rather than compile errors.
findings.recommendation: Replace unreachable!() with explicit match arms or refactor to avoid the need for unreachable entirely. If the invariant is truly impossible to violate, use debug_assert!(false, "...") to catch it only in debug builds, or restructure the match to make the invariant structurally guaranteed.
